field.value: The group of Dionysus with the Satyr (also known as Bacchus and Silenus) derives from a Hellenistic-period work of Greek origin, which reappeared in a Roman copy of the second century CE. The god of wine is represented according to the ideal type of male beauty, with his hand resting on his head. The satyr on the left provides a contrast to the god's drunkenness, holding him up behind his back. This statuary group was found during the excavations for the construction of palazzo Mattei at Quattro Fontane. The discovery of the site on the Quirinal, where Constantine's baths had been might suggest that the statuary group originally decorated a room in the baths.
